original_hypothesis.title: Fee-on-Transfer Tokens Cause Revert in Incentive Distribution
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The addIncentives function records the intended transfer amount rather than the actual received balance. If a fee-on-transfer token is used as an incentive, the contract will hold fewer tokens than recorded, causing transferIncentives to revert and bricking the pool's launch.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: When transferIncentives is called during the pre-launch phase, it attempts to approve and notify the external bribe with the recorded amount. Because the actual balance is lower due to the transfer fee, the transaction will revert. This prevents the pool from transitioning to PRE_LAUNCH, effectively bricking it.

proof_of_signal.pattern: State update uses transfer parameter instead of balance difference

preconditions: Token owner adds a fee-on-transfer token as an incentive

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should calculate balance difference before and after transfer

analysis_questions: Are fee-on-transfer tokens explicitly supported or blocked by the tokenHandler?
Does this revert block the entire checkAtEpochFlip loop for all pools?
validation_requirements: Deploy a fee-on-transfer token, add it as an incentive, and verify transferIncentives reverts

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: A token owner unknowingly (or maliciously) adds a fee-on-transfer token as an incentive. At the epoch flip, the manager attempts to pre-launch the pool, but transferIncentives reverts due to insufficient balance. The pool is permanently stuck.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Medium - Pool is bricked, funds may be stuck until governance intervention
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Depositors in the specific genesis pool
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires governance to manually reject the pool

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for fee-on-transfer tokens in addIncentives

validation_result.impact_summary: addIncentives records transfer parameter instead of actual received balance. Fee-on-transfer tokens cause inflated accounting, making transferIncentives and claimIncentives revert due to insufficient balance, potentially bricking the pool.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The addIncentives function at GenesisPool.sol lines 74-87 uses safeTransferFrom to pull tokens and then records the input _amount parameter directly into the incentives mapping. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the actual amount received by the contract is less than _amount. Later, transferIncentives (lines 141-158) reads the inflated incentives[token] value and calls safeApprove + notifyRewardAmount with this amount. The external bribe's notifyRewardAmount would attempt to transferFrom the GenesisPool for the full recorded amount, but the pool's actual balance is lower, causing revert. This blocks the pool from transitioning to PRE_LAUNCH status. The same issue affects claimIncentives (lines 213-223) which would attempt safeTransfer of the inflated amount. The code lacks the standard balance-before-and-after pattern needed for fee-on-transfer compatibility.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 74-87 of GenesisPool.sol clearly show the pattern: safeTransferFrom is called with _amount, and then incentives[_token] += _amount records the parameter directly without measuring actual balance change. Lines 141-158 show transferIncentives reading the inflated value and attempting to approve/transfer the full recorded amount to external_bribe.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Records _amount (the parameter) directly into incentives mapping instead of measuring actual balance received. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the contract receives less than _amount, creating an inflated accounting entry.

Reads the inflated incentives value and attempts to approve/transfer the full amount. Since the contract holds fewer tokens than recorded, the external bribe's transferFrom will revert due to insufficient balance.
Also affected: attempts to transfer the inflated recorded amount, which exceeds actual balance, causing revert.

calling_sequence: 1. Token owner calls addIncentives with a fee-on-transfer token and amount X
2. safeTransferFrom transfers X tokens but contract receives X-fee (GenesisPool.sol:85)
3. incentives[token] += X records the full amount, not actual received (GenesisPool.sol:89)
4. Later, manager calls depositToken which triggers _preLaunchPool (GenesisPoolManager.sol:154-159)
7. safeApprove(external_bribe, X) sets allowance to X (GenesisPool.sol:153)
8. notifyRewardAmount attempts transferFrom(genesisPool, bribe, X) but pool only has X-fee tokens
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: addIncentives records _amount parameter at line 89 without measuring balance difference. When transferIncentives executes at lines 148-155, it reads the inflated amount and attempts to approve/transfer to external_bribe. The notifyRewardAmount call at line 154 (external bribe implementation, not in scope) would typically pull tokens via transferFrom. Since the GenesisPool's actual balance is less than the recorded amount (by the fee amount), this transferFrom fails. The poolStatus = PRE_LAUNCH at line 157 is never reached.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Fee-on-transfer tokens can be connectors or native tokens
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts specifically address fee-on-transfer token restrictions in tokenHandler. The code only checks tokenHandler.isConnector() which is an external dependency not fully visible in scope.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: A token owner (innocently or maliciously) adds a fee-on-transfer token as an incentive via addIncentives. The fee-on-transfer token must be either the nativeToken or pass the tokenHandler.isConnector check. The recorded amount exceeds the actual balance. When the pool reaches the PRE_LAUNCH transition (either via depositToken triggering _preLaunchPool, or via checkAtEpochFlip), transferIncentives is called. The external bribe's notifyRewardAmount attempts to pull the full recorded amount, but the pool lacks sufficient tokens. The transaction reverts. If triggered via checkAtEpochFlip, the lack of try-catch means the entire epoch flip loop reverts, potentially blocking all genesis pools from launching in that epoch.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Medium-High. If a fee-on-transfer token passes the connector/native token check, the genesis pool becomes stuck and cannot transition to PRE_LAUNCH. When triggered through checkAtEpochFlip, the revert cascades and blocks all genesis pools in that epoch from processing. Recovery would require governance to reject the pool. Funds (native tokens, funding deposits) may be stuck until governance intervenes.
recommended_mitigations: Use the balance-before-and-after pattern in addIncentives: record balanceBefore = IERC20(token).balanceOf(address(this)) before transfer, then actualReceived = IERC20(token).balanceOf(address(this)) - balanceBefore after transfer, and use actualReceived for accounting
Alternatively, explicitly block fee-on-transfer tokens by checking balance difference matches the transfer amount and reverting if not
Add try-catch blocks in checkAtEpochFlip and checkBeforeEpochFlip loops to prevent a single pool failure from blocking all pools
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a fee-on-transfer ERC20 token (e.g., 1% transfer fee). 2. Register it as a connector in tokenHandler or use it as nativeToken. 3. Create a genesis pool and add the fee-on-transfer token as incentive via addIncentives(). 4. Verify incentives[token] > actual token balance of GenesisPool. 5. Trigger transferIncentives (via depositToken or checkAtEpochFlip). 6. Observe the revert when notifyRewardAmount tries to pull the full recorded amount.
uncertainties: The behavior of IBribe.notifyRewardAmount is not visible in scope - it may or may not pull tokens via transferFrom. If it only records the amount without pulling tokens, the revert would instead occur when the bribe tries to distribute rewards later.
Whether fee-on-transfer tokens can actually pass the tokenHandler.isConnector() check depends on the tokenHandler implementation which is not in scope.
additional_testing_needed: Test with actual fee-on-transfer token to confirm revert behavior in transferIncentives
Verify IBribe.notifyRewardAmount implementation to confirm it pulls tokens via transferFrom
Test cascade effect when transferIncentives reverts inside checkAtEpochFlip loop
